I've benchmarked code with `.get()`:
```python
...
expr_cache = cache[id(self)]
node = expr_cache.get(pos, MARKER)
if node is MARKER:
    node = expr_cache[pos] = self._uncached_match(text,
                                                  pos,
                                                  cache,
                                                  error)
...
```
and this modification results in small slowdown:
```
+-----------+--------+----------------------------+
| Benchmark | in     | get                        |
+===========+========+============================+
| timeit    | 111 ms | 118 ms: 1.06x slower (+6%) |
+-----------+--------+----------------------------+
```
I think this happens because most of the time there is no cache entry for current parsing position, so second lookup doesn't performed too often. A trace with line execution count shows this:
```python
36359:         expr_cache = cache[id(self)]
36359:         if pos in expr_cache:
 2738:             node = expr_cache[pos]
               else:
                   # TODO: Set default value to prevent infinite recursion in left-recursive rules.
33621:             node = expr_cache[pos] = self._uncached_match(text,
```

Yeah, that's true. I just pushed a commit that allows you to use the short form of the name or the endpoint_prefix -- e.g., `elb` or `elasticloadbalancing`. We first try to resolve endpoints using the given name. If that result is empty, we then try to load the service data associated with the given service_name to grab it's `endpointPrefix`. If the service cannot be found (e.g., `foo`), then we return an empty list. Otherwise, we return the result of resolving endpoints using the service's `endpointPrefix`.

Yeah. Consider,

    foo(True, False, True)

Versus,

    foo(Incoming, LazyReads, Ephemeral)

With named arguments we can add readability to the former case (`foo(incoming=True, strict_reads=False, ephemeral=True)`) but then sentinel values allow for more than two states (true/false). In this case it doesn't matter much since we'll only ever have two directions, except that maybe sentinel values are slightly more informative (`direction=incoming` vs `incoming=True`).
If you want to guard here this should be an isinstance check (I can pass `True`). I'd also recommend a different exception type.

Please avoid importing `*` from a module. Instead, do `dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta`.

Also, generally, we organize imports into 3 sections: standard library modules, extra modules, and local package modules. Sorted by module name in each section, like

```
# (No standard library modules imported in analytics.py)

import dateutil.parser
import dateutil.relativedelta

from mapbox.services.base import Service
```

Finally, dateutil needs to be added to the `install_requires` list in setup.py. It is a dependency of boto or requests, but if we ever replaced those we'd no longer have a specification of our dependency on dateutil.

So, the current man-page says:

```
      There are several reasons why the linked list may have  more  than  one
       addrinfo structure, including: the network host is multihomed, accessi‐
       ble over multiple protocols (e.g., both AF_INET and AF_INET6);  or  the
       same  service  is available from multiple socket types (one SOCK_STREAM
       address and another SOCK_DGRAM address, for  example).   Normally,  the
       application  should  try using the addresses in the order in which they
       are returned.   The  sorting  function  used  within  getaddrinfo()  is
       defined  in  RFC 3484; the order can be tweaked for a particular system
       by editing /etc/gai.conf (available since glibc 2.5).
```

So the first element should be the best choice when you pick only a single entry.

Since it looks like we cannot get the _best_ address for the server from the list without applying a lot of additional magic, I would recommend I change the code to return all entries. The main code will then try all entries until the DNS request succeeded for one of them. As a benefit, we can also change the configuration file in a way that you can specify multiple IP addresses there and have the additional ones as a fallback.

If the duration is less than 120 seconds, we will attempt to use it for its full duration.  Since we consider the start time to be the time that we first sent the request, that cannot be later than the time the server received the request and created the token.

So, if our clock and BIG-IP's clock are in perfect sync, no issues (and actually in the case of perfect sync, this "renew early" provision is unnecessary).  If our clock is faster than BIG-IP's, also no problem (we'll consider the token expired a little early).

If:
- The BIG-IP is configured to have token durations smaller than 120 seconds, more than 30x smaller than the default.
- Our clock runs slower: it loses X seconds per token duration, where X is the total time it takes us to prepare the request, send it over the network, and for BIG-IP to receive and parse that request and generate a token.
- Clients generate a request right at the end of this token duration.

then we will attempt to use an old token instead of getting a new one, and the request will fail.

This leaves an object loaded in memory in ganga and releases the file locks.
If someone/something/some-event causes the file on disk to change we lose all knowledge of it as we make no attempt to re-load the object.

Worst case scenario this allows for another instance of ganga to grab the job, manipulate it and then this instance to ignore the changes and obliterate them upon a future write action.

If we remove this lock we should fully un-load the job and we currently have no mechanism for this.

This just allows for 2 instances of ganga (launched on purpose or by accident by a user) to get into a very bad state which can be avoided by only releasing objects on exit at the moment.

Isn't `Expires: 0` equivalent to `Cache-Control: no-cache`? The `no-cache` directive does *not* mean don't cache (despite its name), it just means 'revalidate each time', where 'revalidate' means send a conditional request to the server and check that you get a 403 and not a 401 or 200 before serving the data from cache. So this `if / else` statement creates a situation where adding an `collection_expires` will cause the client to not revalidate. Revalidation is useful for making sure that each client receives updates to the resource contents asap, but also for revoking access. So setting `collection_expires` to a date in the future now has the side-effect that you cannot revoke access to the resource until that date.

I reworked some portion of the code and included some selection cuts. 26,517 galaxies  pass the cuts out of the total 28,532 galaxies with I band magnitude <25.2. Previously with `exclusion_level=bad_stamp` I was losing about 10% galaxies. I visually inspected the galaxies and realized that most of these were large galaxies for which HSM adaptive moments failed to converge. I altered the input HSM params and most of these passed, except galaxies with high ellipticities. So I decided to include galaxies with `ELLIPTICITY` >0.75 computed by SExtractor, even if  adaptive moments were not computed; I set `sn_ellip_gauss`= -10 manually.

A galaxy is included in the final catalog if:
1)  `sn_ellip_gauss` > 0 or ELLIPTICITY >0.75, in both filters, and 
2) Closest masked pixel is more than 5 pixels from the center, and
3) If closest masked pixel is closer than 11 pixels, `average_mask_adjacent_pixel_count` must be less than 20% of the brightest pixel.

With these cuts, this is the distribution of galaxies that would make it to the final catalog. The left column is the fraction of galaxies that pass selection cuts as a function of half light radius, ellipticity and position angle (measured by SExtractor), and magnitude. The right column is the histogram of all galaxies(without applying cuts)  as a function of the same parameters.
